title: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections
section: Infections Due to DNA and RNA Respiratory Viruses

items.title: Understanding Arbovirus Extrinsic Incubation Periods
items.content: Arboviruses are transmitted to vertebrates through arthropod vectors, such as mosquitoes. After ingestion of a blood meal from an infected host, the virus undergoes extrinsic incubation within the vector, typically lasting 1–3 weeks in mosquitoes.
keyPoints: Extrinsic incubation period for arboviruses in mosquitoes lasts 1-3 weeks.
Virus dissemination to salivary glands enables transmission during subsequent blood meals.
Arthropod vectors are generally unharmed by the infection.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · Arthropod-Borne and Rodent-Borne Virus Infections

items.title: Characteristics of Rodent-Borne Viruses in Natural Reservoirs
items.content: Rodent-borne viruses maintain a cycle of transmission among rodent populations, with rodents often becoming chronically infected. These infections typically do not cause overt disease in the rodent hosts.
keyPoints: Rodents are natural reservoirs for rodent-borne viruses and become chronically infected.
High degree of rodent-virus specificity is observed.
Overt disease in rodent reservoirs is rare.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · Arthropod-Borne and Rodent-Borne Virus Infections

items.title: Characteristics and Transmission of Togaviridae Alphaviruses
items.content: Members of the family Togaviridae, specifically those in the genus Alphavirus, have linear, positive-sense RNA genomes ranging from approximately 9.7 to 11.8 kb. These viruses form enveloped icosahedral virions with a diameter of about 60–70 nm and are transmitted to vertebrates via mosquitoes.
keyPoints: Togaviridae Alphavirus genome: linear, positive-sense RNA, ~9.7–11.8 kb
Virion structure: enveloped icosahedral, ~60–70 nm diameter
Transmission vector: mosquitoes

items.title: Transmission and Adaptation of Human Mammarenaviruses
items.content: Human-infecting members of the Arenaviridae family are classified under the genus Mammarenavirus. These viruses form spherical to pleomorphic virions (50–300 nm) with two genomic RNAs, small (~3.5 kb) and large (~7.5 kb), encoding proteins in an ambisense orientation. They persist by infecting rodents, which transmit them through aerosolized excreta or direct contact.
keyPoints: Mammarenavirus virion size: 50–300 nm
Genomic RNAs: small (~3.5 kb), large (~7.5 kb)
Transmission: inhalation of rodent excreta, direct contact with rodents
Rodent adaptation: specific to particular rodent types

items.subtopic: ETIOLOGY | BUNYAVIRICETES: HANTAVIRIDAE, NAIROVIRIDAE, PERIBUNYAVIRIDAE, AND PHENUIVIRIDAE
items.title: Transmission Modes and Host Adaptation of Bunyaviricetes Families
items.content: Bunyaviricetes families infecting humans include Hantavirus, Nairovirus, Peribunyavirus, and Phenuivirus. These viruses have three genomic RNAs with sizes ranging from 1–2 kb (S), 3.6–5.3 kb (M), to 6.4–12.3 kb (L). Transmission varies: hantaviruses are rodent-borne, nairoviruses by ticks, peribunyaviruses by mosquitoes, and phenuiviruses through both arthropod vectors and vertebrates.
keyPoints: Genomic RNAs sizes: S (~1–2 kb), M (3.6–5.3 kb), L (6.4–12.3 kb)
Virion size: ~80–120 nm
Transmission modes: rodent-borne (hantaviruses), tick-borne (nairoviruses), mosquito-borne (peribunyaviruses, phenuiviruses)
Vertical transmission in vectors: common for peribunyaviruses and phenuiviruses

items.title: Transmission Mechanisms of Orthoflaviviruses
items.content: Orthoflaviviruses, belonging to the Flaviviridae family, are transmitted among vertebrates by mosquitoes (Aedes and Culex spp.) and ixodid ticks. Humans become accidental hosts through arthropod bites. These viruses have single-stranded positive-sense RNA genomes of approximately 11 kb and form spherical enveloped particles measuring 40–60 nm in diameter.
keyPoints: Orthoflaviviruses are transmitted by mosquitoes (Aedes, Culex spp.) and ixodid ticks.
Humans are accidental hosts, typically infected via arthropod bites.
Genomes: single-stranded positive-sense RNA, approximately 11 kb.
Virion size: spherical enveloped particles, 40–60 nm in diameter.

items.title: Characteristics of Bornavirids and Rhabdovirids
items.content: Bornavirids and rhabdovirids infecting humans have linear non-segmented, negative-sense RNA genomes. Bornavirids possess approximately 9 kb genomes and form spherical particles (90–130 nm in diameter). Rhabdovirids have larger genomes (approximately 11–15 kb) and form bullet-shaped to pleomorphic enveloped particles (100–430 nm long and 45–100 nm wide). The bornavirus genus Orthobornavirus is rodent-borne, transmitted by squirrels. Rhabdovirid genera include Tibrovirus, causing fever and myalgia syndrome, and Vesiculovirus, transmitted by insects.
keyPoints: Bornavirids: linear non-segmented, negative-sense RNA genomes (~9 kb), spherical particles (90–130 nm).
Rhabdovirids: linear non-segmented, negative-sense RNA genomes (~11–15 kb), bullet-shaped to pleomorphic particles (100–430 nm long, 45–100 nm wide).
Orthobornavirus transmitted by squirrels.
Tibrovirus causes fever and myalgia syndrome; Vesiculovirus transmitted by insects.

items.title: Why Geographic Origin is Crucial in Diagnosing Arthropod-Borne and Rodent-Borne Viruses
items.content: A patient's geographic origin or travel history can provide important clues for differential diagnosis of arthropod-borne and rodent-borne viruses, as these infections are generally confined to areas inhabited by their reservoir hosts and/or vectors. Diseases like yellow fever, dengue, chikungunya virus disease, HFRS caused by Seoul virus, sandfly fever, and Oropouche virus disease can be associated with urban outbreaks.
keyPoints: Geographic origin or travel history provides crucial clues for differential diagnosis.
Diseases confined to areas inhabited by reservoir hosts/vectors.
Urban outbreak diseases include yellow fever, dengue (with/without warning signs), severe dengue, chikungunya virus disease, HFRS caused by Seoul virus, sandfly fever, and Oropouche virus disease.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: How Laboratory Diagnosis is Essential for Arthropod-Borne and Rodent-Borne Virus Infections
items.content: While clinical signs and epidemiologic clues may suggest a presumptive diagnosis in epidemic settings, laboratory confirmation is required. Acute-phase serum samples collected within 3 or 4 days of onset can yield virus isolates. Paired serum samples demonstrate rising antibody titers. Rapid tests such as antigen-detection ELISAs, IgM-capture ELISAs, and multiplex PCR assays provide timely diagnosis from a single sample. RT-PCR is particularly sensitive for diagnosing infections without detectable antigens.
keyPoints: Laboratory confirmation required despite clinical signs/epidemiologic clues.
Acute-phase serum samples collected within 3 or 4 days of onset yield isolates.
Paired serum samples show rising antibody titers.
Rapid tests include antigen-detection ELISAs, IgM-capture ELISAs, and multiplex PCR assays.
RT-PCR is sensitive for diagnosing infections without detectable antigens.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · EPIDEMIOLOGY | DIAGNOSIS

items.title: Understanding Syndromic Categories in Arthropod-Borne and Rodent-Borne Viruses
items.content: Arthropod-borne and rodent-borne viral infections can be categorized into five broad syndromes: F&M, A&R, encephalitis, pulmonary disease, or VHF. These categories help guide clinical diagnosis but often overlap in practice due to the complex nature of these diseases.
keyPoints: Five broad syndromic categories: F&M, arthritis and rash (A&R), encephalitis, pulmonary disease, VHF.
Categories often overlap; most severe phenotypes are better known than common manifestations.
Examples include West Nile virus presenting with milder F&M during epidemics, Rift Valley fever more commonly causing F&M syndrome despite being known for VHF.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · CLINICAL DISEASE SYNDROMES

items.title: Dengue Virus Classification and Clinical Management
items.content: The classification of dengue virus infections has evolved to improve clinical management. The WHO now categorizes dengue into 'dengue without warning signs,' 'dengue with warning signs,' and 'severe dengue' to better guide treatment and reporting.
keyPoints: Dengue virus historically classified as 'dengue fever' (F&M syndrome) or 'dengue hemorrhagic fever' including 'dengue shock syndrome'.
WHO revised classification: 'dengue without warning signs,' 'dengue with warning signs,' and 'severe dengue'.
Revised nomenclature aids in clinical management and case reporting, especially important for pediatric populations.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · CLINICAL DISEASE SYNDROMES

items.title: Recognizing F&M as a Prodrome for Severe Syndromes
items.content: Fever and myalgia (F&M) is the most common clinical syndrome following arthropod-borne and rodent-borne infections, often serving as an initial prodrome for more severe syndromes such as arthritic, neuroinvasive, pulmonary, or VHF. Clinicians should monitor patients with F&M for additional symptoms indicating progression to these more serious conditions.
keyPoints: F&M is the most common syndrome after arthropod-borne and rodent-borne infections.
F&M can be a prodrome for arthritic, neuroinvasive, pulmonary, or VHF syndromes.
Symptoms include abrupt onset of fever, chills, intense myalgia, malaise, headache, photophobia, and retroorbital pain.
Physical findings may include conjunctival injection, pharyngeal erythema/exudate, muscle tenderness, abdominal tenderness, and a nonpruritic maculopapular rash.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · FEVER AND MYALGIA (F&M) (part 1)

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of F&M Syndrome
items.content: The nonspecific nature of F&M syndrome necessitates a broad differential diagnosis, including other viral infections, bacterial infections like anicteric leptospirosis and rickettsial diseases, and community-acquired parasitic infections such as malaria. The absence of cough and coryza helps differentiate it from influenza.
keyPoints: F&M syndrome is nonspecific and requires a broad differential diagnosis.
Consider arboviral infections, bacterial infections (e.g., anicteric leptospirosis, rickettsial diseases), and parasitic infections (e.g., malaria).
Influenza is less likely due to the absence of cough and coryza.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · FEVER AND MYALGIA (F&M) (part 1)

items.title: Prevention Strategies for Arthropod-Borne Viral Infections
items.content: Preventive measures against viral infections causing F&M syndrome focus on vector control, including the destruction of mosquito breeding sites and emerging technologies like genetically modified mosquitoes. Personal protective strategies include using window screens, permethrin-impregnated bed nets, arthropod repellents, long-sleeved clothing, and avoiding vector habitats.
keyPoints: Vector control is key to preventing viral infections causing F&M syndrome.
Destruction of mosquito breeding sites is economically sound.
Emerging technologies include genetically modified mosquitoes and Wolbachia bacteria.
Personal protective measures: window screens, permethrin-impregnated bed nets, DEET repellents, long-sleeved clothing, avoiding vector habitats.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · FEVER AND MYALGIA (F&M) (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic Approach and Key Features of Lymphocytic Choriomeningitis
items.content: Lymphocytic choriomeningitis virus (LCM) infection presents with a characteristic gradual onset, distinct from typical F&M syndromes. The initial febrile phase lasts 3-6 days, followed by CNS involvement marked by meningeal and occasionally encephalitic signs. Diagnostic tests include IgM-capture ELISA, RT-PCR for CSF, and serologic testing for both mother and fetus in pregnancy-related cases.
keyPoints: LCM has a gradual onset with initial febrile phase lasting 3–6 days.
CNS involvement includes meningeal signs (severe headache, photophobia) and encephalitic signs (altered mental status).
CSF monocyte counts >1,000/μL and hypoglycorrhachia in one-third of patients are diagnostic clues.
IgM-capture ELISA and RT-PCR for CSF confirm diagnosis; crucial in immunosuppressed or pregnant patients.
Maternal-to-fetal transmission can lead to fetal death or congenital anomalies.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · FEVER AND MYALGIA (F&M) (part 2)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Complications of Oropouche Virus Infection
items.content: Oropouche virus, transmitted by biting midges in urban settings, causes acute febrile disease with a biphasic evolution. While most cases are mild, some patients develop severe neurologic or hemorrhagic syndromes. Pregnant women may experience miscarriage or congenital anomalies. Diagnosis is confirmed via serologic detection (IgM) or PCR.
keyPoints: Oropouche virus causes acute febrile disease with biphasic evolution and potential for neurologic/hemorrhagic complications.
Transmission occurs through biting midges, particularly in urban areas.
Severe cases may involve meningoencephalitis or Guillain-Barré syndrome.
Pregnancy-related infections can lead to miscarriage or congenital anomalies.
Diagnosis is confirmed by serologic detection (IgM) or PCR.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · FEVER AND MYALGIA (F&M) (part 2)

items.title: Differential Diagnosis and Epidemiology of Peribunyavirids
items.content: Peribunyavirids, including Oripouche virus, are common causes of arboviral infections in South American jungles. They present with F&M syndrome, sometimes with severe complications. Iquitos virus is a recently discovered relative that mimics Oropouche virus disease. Diagnosis involves serologic detection or PCR.
keyPoints: Peribunyavirids cause acute febrile disease in South American jungles and urban settings.
Oropouche virus can lead to severe neurologic or hemorrhagic syndromes.
Iquitos virus mimics Oropouche virus disease, complicating differential diagnosis.
Serologic detection (IgM) or PCR confirms acute F&M syndrome.
No specific therapeutics or vaccines are available.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · FEVER AND MYALGIA (F&M) (part 2)

items.title: Phlebovirus Infections: Focus on Toscana Virus
items.content: Toscana virus is the only Phlebovirus transmitted by sandflies known to cause central and peripheral nervous system diseases, including encephalitis, meningitis, or polymyeloradiculopathy. It's prevalent in Mediterranean regions during summer months, affecting rural residents and travelers.
keyPoints: Toscana virus is the only Phlebovirus transmitted by sandflies known to cause CNS disease.
Transmission occurs through bites from Phlebotomus sandflies, with transovarial transmission complicating control efforts.
Outbreaks of aseptic meningitis or meningoencephalitis are associated with Toscana virus.
Clinical presentation includes F&M syndrome onset 3–6 days post-bite, sometimes with a papule at the bite site.
High attack rates among visitors in endemic areas due to lack of protective immunity.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · FEVER AND MYALGIA (F&M) (part 3)

items.title: Emerging Heartland Virus Infection
items.content: Heartland virus, an emerging pathogen in the eastern, midwestern, and southern United States, causes severe infections often fatal among males over 50 with comorbidities. Since its first description in 2012, there have been more than 60 reported cases.
keyPoints: Heartland virus is an emerging pathogen causing severe infection in the U.S., particularly affecting males >50 years old with comorbidities.
>60 cases reported since 2012.
The disease can be fatal, highlighting the need for awareness and early detection.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · FEVER AND MYALGIA (F&M) (part 3)

items.title: Dengue Epidemiology and Transmission
items.content: Dengue, caused by DENV-1-4, is the most prevalent arthropod-borne viral disease worldwide with approximately 400 million infections annually. Over 100 countries are endemic, with Asia bearing 70% of the global burden. The principal vectors are Aedes aegypti mosquitoes, which thrive near human habitation and can transmit the virus year-round in tropical regions.
keyPoints: Approximately 400 million dengue infections occur per year globally.
25% of these infections cause clinical illness.
>100 countries worldwide are endemic for dengue.
Asia bears 70% of the global burden of dengue cases.
Transmission occurs between latitudes 25°N and 25°S, with seasonal spread into the United States and Europe documented.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · FEVER AND MYALGIA (F&M) (part 4)

items.title: Clinical Presentation of Dengue
items.content: Dengue typically presents with sudden onset of high-grade fever, headache, retroorbital pain, back pain, and severe myalgia. A transient macular rash may appear at illness onset, followed by a more widespread rash during deferescence. Patients may also experience anorexia, nausea, vomiting, and cutaneous hypersensitivity.
keyPoints: Dengue presents with sudden high-grade fever, headache, retroorbital pain, back pain, and severe myalgia.
A transient macular rash often appears at illness onset.
During deferescence (days 3–5), a maculopapular rash spreads to the trunk, extremities, and face.
Epistaxis and petechiae may be noted in uncomplicated dengue.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · FEVER AND MYALGIA (F&M) (part 4)

items.title: Laboratory Findings and Diagnosis of Dengue
items.content: Dengue is characterized by leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and modest elevations in serum AST activity. Diagnosis can be confirmed through antigen-detection ELISA or RT-PCR during the acute phase, or IgM ELISA or paired serology during recovery.
keyPoints: Laboratory findings include leukopenia, thrombocytopenia, and modest elevations of serum AST activity.
Diagnosis is made by antigen-detection ELISA or RT-PCR in the acute phase.
IgM ELISA or paired serology can be used during recovery for diagnosis.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · FEVER AND MYALGIA (F&M) (part 4)

items.title: Zika Virus (ZIKV) Epidemiology and Transmission
items.content: Zika virus is an emerging pathogen transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes, with significant outbreaks reported globally since the early 2000s. The disease was first identified in humans during a yellow fever outbreak in Nigeria in 1954. ZIKV infections have been noted to increase rapidly, particularly in regions like Brazil and other parts of the Americas from 2014 onwards.
keyPoints: Zika virus discovered in 1947 in Uganda
First human infection documented in 1954 in Nigeria
Large outbreaks reported on Yap Island (2007), Cambodia (2010), Philippines (2012), French Polynesia (2013-2014)
Disease first reported in the Americas on Easter Island (2014) and Brazil (2015)
Estimated 440,000 to 1.3 million cases in Brazil by end of 2015
ZIKV infections recorded on five continents in 85 countries by May 2017
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · FEVER AND MYALGIA (F&M) (part 5)

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Complications of ZIKV Infection
items.content: Zika virus infections are often asymptomatic or present with mild symptoms, frequently misdiagnosed as dengue or influenza. Common symptoms include low-grade fever, rash, arthralgia/myalgia, conjunctivitis, headache, and malaise. Severe complications can involve neurologic syndromes such as Guillain-Barré syndrome and congenital microcephaly.
keyPoints: Typically asymptomatic or mild
Symptoms: low-grade fever, itchy maculopapular rash, arthralgia/myalgia, conjunctivitis, headache, malaise
Other manifestations: gastrointestinal (nausea, vomiting), genitourinary (hematospermia), cardiac (myocarditis/pericarditis)
Neurologic complications include Guillain-Barré syndrome and congenital microcephaly
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · FEVER AND MYALGIA (F&M) (part 5)

items.title: Transmission Modes of ZIKV
items.content: Zika virus is primarily transmitted through the bite of infected Aedes mosquitoes. However, transmission can also occur via perinatal routes, breastfeeding, sexual contact, blood transfusion, and organ transplantation. The persistence of infectious ZIKV or its RNA in semen has been documented for extended periods post-infection.
keyPoints: Primary transmission: bites by infected Aedes mosquitoes
Other modes: perinatal, breastfeeding, sexual contact, blood transfusion, organ transplantation
ZIKV RNA persistence in semen up to 188 days; infectious virus up to 69 days post-illness onset
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · FEVER AND MYALGIA (F&M) (part 5)

items.title: Colorado Tick Fever: Clinical Presentation and Diagnostic Approach
items.content: Colorado tick fever, a significant spinareovirid arthropod-borne disease transmitted by Rocky Mountain wood ticks, presents with fever, myalgia, headache, and occasionally rash. It can lead to complications such as meningoencephalitis, DIC, pericarditis, myocarditis, orchitis, pulmonary disease, leukopenia, and thrombocytopenia. The biphasic nature of the illness typically lasts 7–10 days.
keyPoints: Transmission by Rocky Mountain wood ticks (Dermacentor andersoni) in mountainous regions at altitudes of 1200–3000 m.
Annual reports: Several hundred cases in the United States and Canada.
Incubation period: Mean of 1–14 days.
Common presentation: Fever, myalgia, headache; rash in a minority.
Complications: Meningoencephalitis (especially in children), DIC with hemorrhagic manifestations, pericarditis, myocarditis, orchitis, pulmonary disease.
Laboratory findings: Leukopenia and thrombocytopenia.
Disease duration: 7–10 days, often biphasic.
Differential diagnosis: Rocky Mountain spotted fever (less common), tularemia.
Diagnostic challenges: Seropositivity may be delayed for 10–14 days; RT-PCR testing recommended early in the disease course.
Viral replication: Detected in erythrocytes, useful in immunofluorescence-stained erythroid smears and blood donor screening.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · FEVER AND MYALGIA (F&M) (part 6)

items.title: Diagnostic Challenges and Considerations for Colorado Tick Fever
items.content: Diagnosing Colorado tick fever can be challenging due to delayed seropositivity, which may not appear until 10–14 days after infection. RT-PCR testing is preferred early in the disease course. The virus's presence in erythrocytes aids diagnosis through immunofluorescence-stained erythroid smears and is crucial for blood donor screening.
keyPoints: Delayed seropositivity: 10–14 days, limiting early serologic testing utility.
RT-PCR testing recommended for early diagnosis.
Viral replication in erythrocytes: Detected via immunofluorescence-stained erythroid smears.
Importance during blood donor screening.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · FEVER AND MYALGIA (F&M) (part 6)

items.title: Chikungunya Virus Disease: Clinical Presentation and Management
items.content: Chikungunya virus disease is characterized by an abrupt onset of fever, severe arthralgia, and constitutional symptoms. It primarily affects adults with a higher incidence in middle-aged to older patients who may develop chronic arthritis.
keyPoints: Incubation period: 2–10 days
Symptoms include severe arthralgia, fever (often with a saddleback pattern), chills, abdominal pain, anorexia, conjunctival injection, headache, nausea, photophobia
Migratory polyarthritis affects small joints of ankles, feet, hands, wrists; larger joints may also be involved
Rash typically appears on the trunk and limbs, may desquamate
Chronic arthritis or arthralgia syndromes common in middle-aged to older patients, particularly those with preexisting osteoarthritis or severe acute disease
Treatment: acetaminophen, NSAIDs; glucocorticoids or DMARDs for refractory arthritis
Live-attenuated vaccine approved by FDA
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ARTHRITIS AND RASH (A&R) (part 1)

items.title: Epidemiology and Transmission of Chikungunya Virus Disease
items.content: Chikungunya virus disease has a global presence, with transmission facilitated by YF mosquitoes and Asian tiger mosquitoes. It affects over 3.6 million cases in the Americas from December 2013 to June 2023.
keyPoints: Epidemic began in Indian Ocean region (Réunion, Mauritius) in 2004
Major vectors: YF mosquitoes, Asian tiger mosquitoes
Transmission documented across Africa, Asia, Europe, Americas, including continental United States
>3.6 million cases reported from 50 countries or territories in the Americas between December 2013 and June 2023
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ARTHRITIS AND RASH (A&R) (part 1)

items.title: Complications and Special Populations in Chikungunya Virus Disease
items.content: Chikungunya virus disease can lead to severe complications, particularly during large outbreaks. Pregnant women face a high risk of maternal-fetal transmission, potentially leading to fetal death.
keyPoints: Severe acute disease may cause cardiorespiratory, neurologic, renal, ocular complications
Deaths typically occur in the context of large outbreaks
Pregnant women at high risk for maternal-fetal transmission; potential for fetal death
Young children develop less prominent symptoms and often have a bullous rash
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ARTHRITIS AND RASH (A&R) (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Epidemiology of Ross River Virus Infection
items.content: Ross River virus (RRV) causes epidemic polyarthritis, with significant outbreaks in Australia and other regions. It is transmitted by specific mosquito species and affects various vertebrate hosts. The disease often presents with symmetric joint pain and a maculopapular rash, which may precede or follow the joint symptoms.
keyPoints: RRV has caused epidemics in Australia since the 20th century, with over 500,000 infections recorded during specific outbreaks.
Transmission is primarily through Aedes normanensis, Aedes vigilax, and Culex annulirostris mosquitoes.
55–75% of human RRV infections are asymptomatic; symptomatic cases involve sudden onset of symmetric joint pain and a nonitchy rash.
Joint involvement can incapacitate patients for 6 months or more, with ankle, knee, wrist, and other joints commonly affected.
Diagnosis relies on serologic tests showing rising antibody titers.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ARTHRITIS AND RASH (A&R) (part 2)

items.title: Distinguishing Features of Barmah Forest Virus Infection
items.content: Barmah Forest virus (BFV) infection presents similarly to Ross River disease but is more likely to involve a rash. It is transmitted by both Aedes and Culex mosquitoes, with horses and possums implicated as hosts.
keyPoints: BFV has been increasingly documented since the 1990s, with over 21,815 cases recorded in Australia from 1991 to 2011.
A nonitchy, diffuse maculopapular rash is more common in BFV infection compared to RRV.
Joint pain and rash symptoms are similar to RRV, but the rash can precede joint pain by several days.
Diagnosis involves serologic tests for rising antibody titers.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ARTHRITIS AND RASH (A&R) (part 2)

items.title: Clinical Course and Management of Sindbis Virus Infection
items.content: Sindbis virus infection is characterized by rash and arthralgia, with symptoms developing after a short incubation period. It affects multiple joints but typically resolves without long-term deformities.
keyPoints: Transmission occurs via Culex and Culiseta mosquitoes across Africa, Asia, Europe, and Australia.
Symptoms include a rash starting on the trunk and spreading to extremities, lasting about 1 week, and polyarticular migratory arthritis.
Constitutional symptoms are mild or absent; fever is modest or not present.
Joint pain may persist for months or years despite lack of deformities.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ARTHRITIS AND RASH (A&R) (part 2)

items.title: Pathogenesis of Arboviral Encephalitis
items.content: Arboviral encephalitides share a similar pathogenesis involving initial viremia from an infected arthropod, leading to multifocal entry into the CNS. This occurs via infection of olfactory neuroepithelium or brain capillary endothelial cells.
keyPoints: Initial viremia originates from the lymphoid system.
Multifocal CNS entry is presumed through olfactory neuroepithelium or infected macrophages.
Infection can also occur in brain capillary endothelial cells.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of Arboviral Encephalitis
items.content: Patients with arboviral encephalitis present with a prodrome of nonspecific symptoms, followed by CNS dysfunction signs. Diagnosis involves history-taking, physical examination, and specific laboratory testing.
keyPoints: Prodrome includes fever, abdominal pain, sore throat, respiratory signs, headache, meningeal signs, photophobia, vomiting.
CNS involvement may lead to lethargy, somnolence, cognitive deficits, disorientation, coma.
CSF examination shows modest WBC increase; glucose is generally normal.
Serum and CSF should be tested for IgM antibodies; RT-PCR can detect viruses.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 1)

items.title: Differential Diagnosis and Management of Arboviral Encephalitis
items.content: Clinicians must consider differential diagnoses such as bacterial meningitis, intoxication, metabolic or oncologic causes. Empirical treatment with acyclovir for herpesvirus and antibiotics for bacterial meningitis is recommended until test results are available.
keyPoints: Consider empirical acyclovir for herpesvirus meningoencephalitis.
Antibiotics should be used for suspected bacterial meningitis.
Exclude intoxication, metabolic or oncologic causes.
Differential diagnosis includes leptospirosis, neurosyphilis, Lyme disease.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 1)

items.title: Imaging Utility in Arboviral Encephalitides
items.content: Medical imaging for arboviral encephalitides is generally nonspecific, but can help rule out other causes. CT and MRI may show normal results or evidence of preexisting conditions/occasional diffuse edema. Characteristic abnormalities include focal changes in eastern equine encephalitis and hemorrhagic bilateral thalamic changes in severe Japanese encephalitis.
keyPoints: Imaging is generally nonspecific for arboviral encephalitides.
CT/MRI may show normal results or preexisting conditions/occasional diffuse edema.
Characteristic imaging: focal abnormalities (eastern equine), hemorrhagic bilateral thalamic changes (severe Japanese).
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 2)

items.title: Clinical Course and Management of Tick-Borne Encephalitides
items.content: Tick-borne encephalitis virus (TBEV) infections have distinct clinical courses based on the subtype. Western/European viruses cause bimodal disease with a febrile prodrome followed by neuroinvasive symptoms, while Far Eastern viruses present more abruptly and severely. Management focuses on supportive care due to lack of specific therapies.
keyPoints: Western/European TBEV: bimodal disease; CFR 1–2%.
Far Eastern TBEV: abrupt/severe course; CFR 20–40%; common sequelae include lower motor neuron paralyses.
Supportive care is crucial due to lack of specific therapies.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 2)

items.title: Diagnostic Approach for Tick-Borne Encephalitides
items.content: Diagnosis of tick-borne encephalitides relies on PCR or virus isolation from blood during early illness. After CNS involvement, diagnosis requires detection of IgM antibodies in serum and/or CSF.
keyPoints: Early stage: Virus detected by PCR or isolated from blood.
After CNS symptoms: Diagnosis via IgM antibodies in serum/CSF.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 2)

items.title: Vaccination Strategy for Central European/Far Eastern Tick-Borne Encephalitis
items.content: Effective vaccination against Central European/Far Eastern tick-borne encephalitis involves alum-adjuvanted, formalin-inactivated virus vaccines. Two doses of the Austrian vaccine are recommended 1–3 months apart or 2 weeks apart for similar antibody responses.
keyPoints: Alum-adjuvanted, formalin-inactivated virus vaccines (FSME-IMMUN and Encepur) available in Austria, Germany, Russia
Two doses of the Austrian vaccine effective when given 1–3 months or 2 weeks apart
Vaccination recommended for individuals with likely rural exposure in endemic areas during transmission season
Postvaccination Guillain-Barré syndrome is a rare complication
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 3)

items.title: Japanese Encephalitis Epidemiology and Clinical Presentation
items.content: Japanese encephalitis, the most significant viral encephalitis in Asia, results in ≈68,000 cases and ≈13,600–20,400 deaths annually. It is transmitted by Culex tritaeniorhynchus mosquitoes, often found near irrigated rice fields.
keyPoints: ≈68,000 annual cases and ≈13,600–20,400 deaths
Endemic in Asia including Russia Far East, Japan, China, India, Pakistan, Southeastern Asia
Transmission by Culex tritaeniorhynchus mosquitoes near irrigated rice fields
Clinical signs range from nonspecific febrile illness to severe encephalitis with high CFR (≈20–30%)
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 3)

items.title: Prevention and Transmission of St. Louis Encephalitis
items.content: St. Louis encephalitis is transmitted by Culex tarsalis mosquitoes in rural areas and urban vectors like Culex pipiens and Culex quinquefasciatus, causing epidemics. Prevention includes using screens and repellents.
keyPoints: Transmitted by Culex tarsalis in rural areas; Culex pipiens and Culex quinquefasciatus in urban settings
Most cases occur June through October in the U.S., with sporadic cases year-round in Latin/Central America and Caribbean
Urban mosquito breeding sites include stagnant water and sewage
Prevention involves window screens, personal protective measures, and repellents
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 3)

items.title: Age-related Susceptibility to Severe Encephalitis
items.content: St. Louis encephalitis and West Nile virus infections demonstrate increased severity of disease with advancing age, despite similar infection rates across all age groups.
keyPoints: St. Louis encephalitis: Most infections are subclinical; severe cases primarily affect the elderly, with a case fatality rate (CFR) of approximately 7%, increasing to about 20% in patients over 60 years old.

West Nile virus: Neuroinvasive disease is more common among the elderly, male, diabetic, hypertensive patients, and those with previous CNS disease. The overall CFR for neuroinvasive cases was reported as part of a larger dataset from 1999 to 2022.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 4)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and CSF Findings in St. Louis Encephalitis
items.content: Patients with St. Louis encephalitis typically present with a nonspecific prodrome followed by rapid-onset CNS manifestations, with characteristic CSF findings.
keyPoints: Prodromal symptoms include fever, malaise, myalgia, headache; CNS symptoms may involve nuchal rigidity, hypotonia, hyperreflexia, myoclonus, and tremors.
CSF analysis typically shows tens to hundreds of WBCs with lymphocytic predominance and a left shift; CSF glucose concentration remains normal.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 4)

items.title: Genetic Risk Factors in WNV Encephalitis
items.content: Chemokine receptor CCR5 deficiency and preexisting autoantibodies against type I interferons are significant genetic risk factors for neuroinvasive disease and death in West Nile Virus (WNV) encephalitis.
keyPoints: Approximately 35% prevalence of preexisting autoantibodies against type I interferons in patients with WNV encephalitis.
These predispositions may underlie about 40% of cases of WNV encephalitis.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 5)

items.title: Clinical Features and Sequelae of LACV Encephalitis
items.content: La Crosse Virus (LACV) encephalitis presents with a range from mild to severe neuroinvasive disease, primarily affecting children under 15 years old.
keyPoints: Neuroinvasive disease after LACV infection varies from aseptic meningitis to severe and occasionally fatal encephalitis (CFR <0.5%).
Common clinical manifestations include headache, lethargy, nausea, vomiting, convulsions in half of patients, and coma in one-third.
Approximately 10% develop postacute sequelae such as recurrent seizures, focal neurologic deficits, and cognitive/behavioral problems.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 5)

items.title: Diagnostic Approach for LACV Encephalitis
items.content: Diagnosis of La Crosse Virus encephalitis relies on specific virologic testing, including IgM capture assays in serum and CSF.
keyPoints: CSF leukocyte counts typically range from 30–500/μL with a monocyte predominance.
WBC count is commonly elevated and left-shifted, sometimes reaching 20,000/μL.
Specific virologic diagnosis based on IgM capture assays of serum and CSF is efficient.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 5)

items.title: Management of Encephalitis: Supportive Care and Specific Treatments
items.content: Supportive care is crucial during the acute phase of encephalitis, focusing on managing complications such as status epilepticus, cerebral edema, and SIADH. While specific treatments like IV ribavirin for LACV are limited due to adverse effects, supportive measures remain central.
keyPoints: Supportive care over 1-2 weeks is essential.
Management includes prevention of status epilepticus, cerebral edema, and SIADH.
IV ribavirin trial in children with LACV was discontinued due to adverse effects.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 6)

items.title: Chandipura Virus: Clinical Presentation and High Mortality
items.content: Chandipura virus, primarily found in India, presents as a rapid-onset influenza-like illness progressing to encephalitis with high mortality. The disease is characterized by fever, headache, abdominal pain, nausea, vomiting, altered mental status, and seizures, with no detectable virus or abnormal cells in CSF.
keyPoints: Chandipura virus causes rapid-onset influenza-like illness followed by encephalitis.
Mortality rate is approximately 45-80%.
No virus or abnormal cells detected in CSF; CNS disease may relate to cerebrovascular events.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 6)

items.title: Eastern Equine Encephalitis: Epidemiology and Severity
items.content: Eastern equine encephalitis is a severe, often fatal disease with high residual postacute sequelae. It occurs primarily in swampy areas of the U.S., transmitted by mosquitoes from birds to mammals. Neuroinvasive disease develops in 2% of adults and 6% of children, with a case fatality rate of ≥30-50%. No specific treatment is available.
keyPoints: Occurs mainly along the east coast of the U.S., with cases (4-40/year).
Neuroinvasive disease develops in 2% of adults and 6% of children.
Case fatality rate ≥30-50%, >50% survivors have residual sequelae.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 6)

items.title: Understanding the Epidemiology and Transmission of VEE
items.content: Epizootic viruses, such as Venezuelan equine encephalitis (VEE), have unknown natural cycles but cause significant outbreaks in equids and humans. These outbreaks are driven by high-level viremia in horses and mules, which transmit the virus to mosquitoes that then infect humans. Human-to-human transmission is unclear.
keyPoints: Epizootics occur every <10 years since 1930s until 1969 with a major outbreak spreading from Central America to southern Texas by 1971.
Genetic sequencing linked the 1969 outbreak to residual 'un-inactivated' IA/B-subtype virus in veterinary vaccines.
Live-attenuated vaccine (TC-83) used to terminate the outbreak in Texas.
Subsequent major outbreaks occurred in 1995 and 1996 with >85,000 clinical cases; 4% had neurologic symptoms/signs resulting in 300 deaths.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 7)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Management of VEE
items.content: During epizootics, human infection is common with clinical disease occurring in 10–60% of infected individuals. Most infections result in acute febrile syndromes, while neurologic disease occurs in 5–15%. Small-molecule and monoclonal antibody-based antiviral therapeutics are under development.
keyPoints: Clinical disease occurs in 10–60% of infected individuals during epizootics.
Most infections result in acute febrile syndromes; neurologic disease occurs in 5–15%.
Low CNS invasion rate supported by absence of encephalitis after aerosol exposure in labs.
Preventive vaccination for horses with TC-83 or inactivated vaccines is crucial.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 7)

items.title: Characteristics and Prevention of Western Equine Encephalitis
items.content: Western equine encephalitis virus involves a maintenance cycle with mosquitoes and birds. Human and equid infections result in encephalitis without amplifying the virus. Incidence has declined due to mosquito management and pesticide use.
keyPoints: Primary maintenance cycle involves Aedes, C. tarsalis, Culiseta mosquitoes, and birds like sparrows and finches.
Equids and humans suffer encephalitis but do not amplify the virus.
Epidemics were common from 1930s to 1950s; only 640 cases reported in the U.S. from 1964 through 2010.
Incidence decline may be due to mosquito management and pesticide use.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 7)

items.title: Recognizing the Prodrome and Pulmonary Phase of Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome
items.content: HPS begins with a nonspecific prodrome followed by pulmonary symptoms. Early recognition is crucial due to rapid progression to respiratory failure.
keyPoints: Incubation period: 2–3 weeks
Prodrome duration: ~3–4 days (range 1–11 days)
Symptoms: fever, malaise, myalgia, gastrointestinal disturbances
Pulmonary phase signs: slightly lowered blood pressure, tachycardia, tachypnea, mild hypoxemia, thrombocytopenia
Radiographic signs: early pulmonary edema
Critical for early recognition and triage
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · PULMONARY DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome
items.content: HPS should be differentiated from other severe acute respiratory infections, informed by local epidemiology.
keyPoints: Considerations: sepsis syndromes, atypical pneumonia (Legionella, Mycoplasma, Q fever), influenza, rickettsial disease
Other differentials: meningococcemia, septicemic plague, tularemia, leptospirosis, dengue, YF
Diagnostic aid: antigen-specific IgM in acute-phase serum
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · PULMONARY DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Radiographic and Laboratory Features of Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome
items.content: Characteristic radiographic and laboratory findings help diagnose HPS, especially during the pulmonary phase.
keyPoints: Radiographic signs: interstitial edema progressing to bilateral alveolar infiltrates with a normal-sized heart
Laboratory findings: thrombocytopenia, circulating atypical lymphocytes (lymphoblasts), left shift with leukocytosis
Additional aids: hemoconcentration, hypoalbuminemia, proteinuria
Severe cases: acidosis, elevated serum lactate, oliguria, decreased glomerular filtration
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · PULMONARY DISEASE (part 1)

items.title: Management of Hemodynamic, Respiratory, and Renal Dysfunction in Pulmonary Disease
items.content: Early triage-based monitoring and management are crucial for improving outcomes in patients with pulmonary disease. Hypotension and hemoconcentration indicate intravascular volume depletion, but aggressive IV fluid therapy should be avoided due to risks of widespread capillary leak, depressed cardiac output, and oliguria.
keyPoints: Early triage-based monitoring is critical for improved outcomes.
Hypotension and hemoconcentration suggest intravascular volume depletion.
Aggressive IV fluid therapy is not advised in the presence of capillary leak, depressed cardiac output, and oliguria.
Mild hypoxemia can be managed with oxygen administration alone.
Mechanical ventilatory support may be needed for respiratory failure and shock.
Pulmonary artery catheterization helps guide inotropic and vasopressor support.
Extracorporeal membrane oxygenation is beneficial before the onset of shock in critically ill patients.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · PULMONARY DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Efficacy and Management Strategies for Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome
items.content: Despite appropriate management, the case fatality rate (CFR) remains high at approximately 30–40%. Most patients who survive the first 48 hours are extubated and discharged within a few days without long-term sequelae. Ribavirin has shown in vitro efficacy against orthohantaviruses but failed to demonstrate effectiveness for HPS in an underpowered trial.
keyPoints: Case fatality rate (CFR) is approximately 30–40% even with appropriate management.
Most patients surviving the first 48 hours are extubated and discharged within a few days without long-term sequelae.
Ribavirin inhibits orthohantaviruses in vitro but failed to show efficacy for HPS in an underpowered trial.
Preclinical development of novel therapeutics, including small-molecule and monoclonal antibody-based treatments, is ongoing.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · PULMONARY DISEASE (part 2)

items.title: Understanding VHF: Hemorrhagic Manifestations and Vascular Dysfunction
items.content: Viral Hemorrhagic Fevers (VHFs) are characterized by severe disease and multisystem organ dysfunction due to vascular instability. Although hemorrhagic manifestations are not always the most prominent feature, they indicate significant vascular dysfunction or damage.
keyPoints: Hemorrhagic signs such as cutaneous flushing and conjunctival suffusion are common due to microvascular dysfunction.
Subtle local hemorrhages (e.g., bleeding gums) can be observed on careful examination.
Circulatory dysfunction contributes to organ-specific damage, with kidneys targeted in HFRS and liver in YF.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Presentation of VHF: Acute Phase Symptoms
items.content: The acute phase of VHF is marked by nonspecific symptoms such as fever and arthralgia/myalgia, often with abrupt onset. As the disease progresses, patients exhibit severe fatigue, prostration, and systemic symptoms.
keyPoints: Initial symptoms include fever, arthralgia/myalgia, severe fatigue, anorexia, headache, sore throat, chest pain, gastrointestinal issues, dizziness, hyperesthesia, and photophobia.
Physical examination may reveal conjunctival suffusion, muscle or abdominal tenderness, borderline hypotension, tachycardia, petechiae, flushing, periorbital edema, and proteinuria.
AST activities are typically elevated at presentation.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 1)

items.title: Diagnostic Clues for VHF: Travel History and Differential Diagnosis
items.content: A key diagnostic clue for VHF is recent travel to an endemic or outbreak area. Rural exposures are more commonly associated with VHFs, except for certain viruses like Seoul virus, DENV-1-4, and YFV.
keyPoints: Travel history within the incubation period is crucial for diagnosis.
Differential diagnoses include falciparum malaria, shigellosis, typhoid fever, leptospirosis, relapsing fever, and rickettsial diseases.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 1)

items.title: Management of Viral Hemorrhagic Fever (VHF) Patients
items.content: Early recognition and appropriate supportive care are critical in managing VHF. This includes isolation, hospitalization, monitoring vital signs, managing dehydration, hypovolemia, hemodynamic instability with IV fluids and vasopressors, preventing acute kidney injury, treating electrolyte abnormalities, addressing concurrent infections, replacing blood products as needed, and taking precautionary measures for hemorrhagic diatheses.
keyPoints: Prompt isolation and hospitalization are essential even without clinical signs.
Management includes prevention of dehydration, hypovolemia, and hemodynamic instability with IV fluids and vasopressors.
DIC treatment should be based on clear laboratory evidence if monitoring is feasible; no proven benefit otherwise.
VHF patients often require intravascular fluids due to insensible fluid losses but may also need inotropic/vasopressor support due to decreased cardiac output, capillary leak syndromes, and oliguria.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 2)

items.title: Specific Therapy for Viral Hemorrhagic Fevers
items.content: Certain VHFs have specific therapies available. For South American VHFs caused by mammarenaviruses, convalescent plasma is effective if administered within the first 8 days of illness, though it may lead to neurologic sequelae. IV ribavirin can be used off-label in the absence of passive antibody therapy. A live-attenuated vaccine for Argentinian hemorrhagic fever has significantly reduced its incidence.
keyPoints: Convalescent plasma is effective within the first 8 days of illness but may cause neurologic sequelae.
IV ribavirin, recommended for Lassa fever (LF), is likely effective against South American VHFs caused by mammarenaviruses.
A live-attenuated vaccine exists for Argentinian hemorrhagic fever and has markedly decreased its incidence.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 2)

items.title: Epidemiology and Clinical Features of Argentinian and Bolivian Hemorrhagic Fevers
items.content: Argentinian hemorrhagic fever, caused by Junin virus, and Bolivian hemorrhagic fever, caused by Machupo virus, have high case fatality rates (CFR) exceeding 50% in outbreaks. Both diseases present similarly but differ epidemiologically due to rodent reservoirs. Transmission is rare but possible person-to-person or nosocomially. Thrombocytopenia and CNS dysfunction are common, with some cases following a neurologic course.
keyPoints: CFR can exceed 50% in outbreaks of Argentinian and Bolivian hemorrhagic fevers.
Both diseases have similar clinical presentations but differ in epidemiology due to rodent reservoirs.
Thrombocytopenia is common, mucosal hemorrhage occurs frequently, and CNS dysfunction (20-30%) is more prevalent.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 2)

items.title: Clinical Progression and Red Flags in Lassa Fever
items.content: Lassa fever typically begins with a nonspecific febrile prodrome, followed by symptoms like sore throat, cough, chest/back pain, and gastrointestinal issues. Disease progression may include conjunctival infection, edema, respiratory/cardiac manifestations, and multisystem organ dysfunction. Key red flags for severe disease include CNS features, hypotension, hemorrhage, jaundice, high serum creatinine levels, elevated AST activity, leukocytosis, and thrombocytopenia.
keyPoints: Gradual onset of illness with nonspecific febrile prodrome
Progression includes sore throat, cough, chest/back pain, gastrointestinal symptoms
Maculopapular rash in light-skinned patients
Conjunctival infection, head/neck edema, respiratory/cardiac manifestations
Multisystem organ dysfunction with acute kidney injury, CNS manifestations, shock, hemorrhage (15–30% of LF patients)
Laboratory abnormalities: leukocytosis, thrombocytopenia, elevated serum creatinine, hyperkalemia, hypoalbuminemia, elevated AST/ALT, proteinuria

Red flags for fatal outcome: high viral load, age, pregnancy status, CNS features, face/neck swelling, hypotension, hemorrhage/jaundice, high serum creatinine, elevated AST, leukocytosis, thrombocytopenia
Pregnancy associated with higher case fatality (90% fetal death in last trimester)
Post-acute sequelae: 30% sensorineural deafness
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 3)

items.title: Management and Treatment of Lassa Fever
items.content: Lassa fever management involves aggressive supportive and critical care, including renal replacement therapy. Ribavirin is used off-label for treatment, with a specific dosing regimen: 32 mg/kg IV initially, followed by 16 mg/kg every 6 hours for 4 days, then 8 mg/kg every 8 hours for 6 days. Side effects such as reversible anemia and bone marrow suppression should be monitored.
keyPoints: Aggressive supportive care required
Ribavirin used off-label: IV route preferred
Dosing regimen: 32 mg/kg initially, then 16 mg/kg every 6 hours for 4 days, followed by 8 mg/kg every 8 hours for 6 days
Side effects: reversible anemia, dependent hemolytic anemia, bone marrow suppression
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 3)

items.title: Epidemiology and Clinical Features of Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome
items.content: Hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome (HFRS) is significant in Asia, with over 100,000 severe cases annually. It's caused by various hantaviruses, including Puumala, Dobrava, and Hantaan viruses. Classic HFRS features include fever, hypotension, hemorrhage, and acute kidney injury, progressing through four stages after a 1- to 2-week incubation period.
keyPoints: >100,000 severe cases of HFRS annually in Asia
Caused by Puumala, Dobrava, Hantaan viruses (and others less frequently)
Classic features: fever, hypotension, hemorrhage, acute kidney injury
Disease progresses through four stages after 1-2 week incubation
Human infection primarily via aerosols of rodent urine
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 3)

items.title: Recognizing the Febrile Stage of Viral Hemorrhagic Fever
items.content: The febrile stage is marked by abrupt onset of fever, severe myalgia, headache, and gastrointestinal symptoms. Key features include photophobia, retroorbital pain, flushing, petechiae, and back pain due to retroperitoneal edema.
keyPoints: Abrupt onset of fever, headache, severe myalgia
Photophobia, retroorbital pain, blurred vision with ciliary body inflammation
Flushing over face, anterior neck, and back; pharyngeal injection, periorbital edema, conjunctival suffusion
Petechiae in pressure areas, conjunctivae, axillae
Back pain due to retroperitoneal edema
Thrombocytopenia and mild to moderate DIC
Early laboratory findings: proteinuria, active urinary sediment
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 4)

items.title: Identifying the Hypotensive Stage in Viral Hemorrhagic Fever
items.content: During this stage, blood pressure drops with compensatory tachycardia. Marked kinin activation and capillary leak lead to hemoconcentration. Renal perfusion issues cause tubular necrosis and oliguria.
keyPoints: Blood pressure falls with compensatory tachycardia
Kinin activation and capillary leak result in hemoconcentration
Leukocytosis with a left shift, persistent thrombocytopenia
Atypical lymphocytes (activated CD8⁺ and CD4⁺ T cells)
Renal perfusion compromised; tubular necrosis at corticomedullary junction
Oliguria, specific gravity of urine falls to 1.010, marked proteinuria
Diffuse hemorrhage: hematemesis, hemoptysis, melena
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 4)

items.title: Management and Diagnostic Approach for Hemorrhagic Fever with Renal Syndrome (HFRS)
items.content: Prompt recognition of HFRS allows for effective management, including shock control and renal support. Diagnosis is confirmed by IgM-capture ELISA or RT-PCR. Ribavirin reduces CFR if administered early.
keyPoints: Suspect HFRS in patients with exposure in endemic areas
Clinical parameters: leukocytosis (left shift), thrombocytopenia, elevated serum creatinine, proteinuria, hematuria
Diagnosis by IgM-capture ELISA or RT-PCR of renal biopsy/thrombus/postmortem tissues
Management includes vasopressor support, crystalloid infusion, IV human serum albumin, timely renal replacement therapy
Control hypertension to prevent intracranial hemorrhage
Ribavirin reduces CFR and morbidity if started within first 4 days of illness
CFR approaches 15% but should be lower than 5% with adequate care
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 4)

items.title: Clinical Course and Prognostic Indicators of Viral Hemorrhagic Fever
items.content: Patients with viral hemorrhagic fever (VHF) typically experience a sudden-onset febrile prodrome followed by severe illness. The disease can rapidly progress to fatal outcomes, particularly in the second week due to multiorgan dysfunction and profound coagulopathy. Key predictors of fatal outcome include clinical features like hematemesis, melena, altered mental status, laboratory biomarkers such as viral load, thrombocytopenia, elevated AST/ALT levels, DIC, and delayed CCHFV-specific IgM and IgG responses.
keyPoints: Incubation period: 1–3 days (tick bite), 3–7 days (blood/body fluids)
Severe illness features: profound inflammatory response, hemorrhagic manifestations, liver injury
Predictors of fatal outcome: hematemesis, melena, altered mental status; viral load, thrombocytopenia, elevated AST/ALT levels, DIC
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 5)

items.title: Treatment Approaches for Viral Hemorrhagic Fever
items.content: The primary treatment strategy for VHF is supportive and critical care, including extracorporeal support for organ dysfunction. Current evidence does not support the use of IV ribavirin due to confounding factors in observational studies. Emerging treatments like small-molecule antivirals (favipiravir and molnupiravir), monoclonal antibodies, and convalescent plasma are under preclinical evaluation but lack clinical trial data. The routine use of steroids or IVIG is not recommended.
keyPoints: Mainstay treatment: supportive and critical care
Ribavirin: no benefit in randomized trials
Emerging treatments: favipiravir, molnupiravir, monoclonal antibodies, convalescent plasma (preclinical evaluation)
Steroids/IVIG: not recommended
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 5)

items.title: Clinical Syndromes and Pathophysiology of Rift Valley Fever Virus
items.content: Rift Valley fever virus (RVFV) causes multiple clinical syndromes. Most infections are subclinical or present as a febrile illness with mild symptoms. A small proportion (<1%) develop severe VHF characterized by liver involvement, rapid progression, and high case fatality (50% within 3–6 days). Less than 1% of patients experience late-onset meningoencephalitis, while about 10% may develop retinal vasculitis leading to vision issues. The late onset of these conditions suggests an immunopathological mechanism.
keyPoints: Most infections: subclinical or mild febrile illness
<1% severe VHF: prominent liver involvement, rapid progression, 50% case fatality within 3–6 days
<1% meningoencephalitis: late onset (1–4 weeks), not usually fatal but with neurologic sequelae
10% retinal vasculitis: late onset (1–3 weeks), potential vision loss
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 5)

items.title: RVF Prevention and Vaccine Limitations
items.content: No proven therapy exists for Rift Valley Fever (RVF). The primary prevention strategy is vaccination of livestock. Human vaccines, such as the formal-inactivated vaccine requiring three doses, are limited in availability and recommended mainly for high-risk groups like laboratory workers and veterinarians in endemic regions.
keyPoints: No proven therapy for RVF
Prevention through livestock vaccination
Formal-inactivated human vaccine requires three injections
Limited quantities of human vaccines
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 6)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Management of Severe Fever with Thrombocytopenia Syndrome (SFTS)
items.content: SFTSV, transmitted by the Asian long-horned tick, presents as a range from mild febrile illness to severe viral hemorrhagic fever with high case fatality rates (>12%) in older individuals. Diagnosis is confirmed via RT-PCR; supportive care is the mainstay of treatment due to lack of approved therapeutics.
keyPoints: SFTSV transmitted by Haemaphysalis longicornis
Clinical range from mild fever to severe VHF with >12% CFR in older patients
Diagnosis via RT-PCR
Supportive care as primary treatment
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 6)

items.title: Recognition and Management of Severe Dengue
items.content: Severe dengue is characterized by plasma leakage leading to shock or respiratory distress, severe bleeding, or organ dysfunction. Early recognition and supportive care are crucial before the critical phase develops.
keyPoints: Criteria for severe dengue include shock, severe bleeding, or organ dysfunction
Early recognition and supportive care essential
Assessment for warning signs in all dengue cases
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 6)

items.title: Risk Factors for Severe Dengue
items.content: Severe dengue progression is influenced by host and viral factors, with immune-mediated enhancement playing a central role. Reinfection with heterotypic DENV serotypes after initial infection can lead to severe dengue.
keyPoints: Host factors include age (risk decreases significantly after 12 years), sex (females more affected than males), race (whites more often affected than blacks), and nutritional status.
Viral factors involve the sequence of infections, e.g., DENV-1 followed by DENV-2 is more severe than DENV-4 followed by DENV-2.
Approximately 500,000 cases of severe dengue occur annually with a case fatality rate (CFR) of about 2.5%.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 7)

items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of Severe Dengue
items.content: Severe dengue is characterized by vascular leak syndrome, leading to shock and other complications. Diagnosis involves virologic and serologic testing.
keyPoints: Symptoms include restlessness, lethargy, thrombocytopenia (<100,000/μL), hemoconcentration, rash, and possibly shock with narrowed pulse pressure.
Vascular leak syndrome results in intravascular volume depletion, hypoalbuminemia, serosal effusions, and circulatory collapse.
Diagnosis is confirmed by nucleic acid amplification or antigen detection within the first 5 days; serologic testing follows, with IgG indicating secondary infection.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 7)

items.title: Management of Severe Dengue
items.content: Early hospitalization and fluid management are critical in severe dengue treatment to prevent shock and manage complications.
keyPoints: Patients should be hospitalized, monitored for hemodynamic instability and bleeding, and receive IV fluids early.
Algorithmic approaches guide fluid management (crystalloid vs colloid vs blood) based on clinical parameters.
With timely recognition and quality treatment, the case fatality rate at experienced centers is as low as 1%.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 7)

items.title: Dengue Vaccine Considerations
items.content: The CYD-TDV vaccine for dengue, licensed in 2015, is associated with increased risk of severe disease in seronegative individuals. Strategic revisions now include prevaccination serologic screening to prevent vaccination of seropositive individuals.
keyPoints: CYD-TDV (Dengvaxia) was licensed in 2015 for ages 9–45 years.
Protection from severe dengue is only observed in previously seropositive individuals.
Risk of severe dengue increased in seronegative vaccine recipients compared to nonvaccinated seronegative individuals.
Prevaccination serologic screening recommended to avoid vaccination of seropositive individuals.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 8)

items.title: Yellow Fever Transmission and Control
items.content: Urban yellow fever transmission is limited to some African cities, but poses a threat in South American cities due to reinfestation by YF mosquitoes. Despite an effective vaccine, significant annual cases occur.
keyPoints: Urban YFV transmission occurs only in some African cities.
Reinfestation of YF mosquitoes in South America poses a threat.
84,000–170,000 severe jungle and urban YF cases occurred in Africa in 2013.
A highly effective and safe vaccine exists.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 8)

items.title: Emerging Dengue Vaccines
items.content: New dengue vaccines like Qdenga and Butantan-DV are being evaluated. Qdenga shows efficacy against DENV-1, -2, but not -3 or -4; Butantan-DV's efficacy against DENV-3 and -4 is unknown.
keyPoints: Qdenga (TAK-3) approved in several European countries for children.
Efficacy against DENV-1 and DENV-2, but not DENV-3 or DENV-4.
Butantan-DV prevents symptomatic DENV-1 and DENV-2 infection; efficacy against DENV-3 and -4 unknown.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 8)

items.title: Yellow Fever: Clinical Course and Prognosis
items.content: Yellow fever (YF) is a viral hemorrhagic fever characterized by an initial febrile illness followed by potential severe hepatic dysfunction. After an incubation period of 3–6 days, patients experience nonspecific symptoms such as fatigue, myalgia, and headaches. Viremia typically lasts 3–4 days, after which some patients develop a toxic phase with significant liver and organ dysfunction.
keyPoints: Incubation period: 3–6 days
Viremia duration: 3–4 days
Toxic phase mortality rate: 50% of affected patients within 7–10 days
Symptoms include jaundice, bleeding, abdominal pain, hyperammonemic encephalopathy, oliguria, azotemia, albuminuria, myocardial injury, and encephalitis
Liver function tests show elevations in hepatic aminotransferase activities to severe liver failure
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 9)

items.title: Yellow Fever Vaccination: Indications and Considerations
items.content: The live-attenuated YF vaccine is crucial for preventing urban yellow fever, especially in visitors to endemic areas. It provides immunity within 10 days that lasts at least 25–35 years. However, vaccination requires careful consideration of contraindications such as egg allergy and age-related risks.
keyPoints: Vaccine: Live-attenuated variant 17D
Immunity duration: At least 25–35 years
Seroprotection rate in adults from non-endemic areas: >90% after one dose
Contraindications: Egg allergy, children <6 months (contraindicated), infants 6–8 months (not recommended unless high risk)
Elderly and those with impaired viral control (e.g., mutations affecting IFNa signaling) require careful risk-benefit assessment
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 9)

items.question: What was the origin of the virus in the massive VEE outbreak that spread throughout Central America and Mexico in 1969?
options: It originated from residual 'un-inactivated' IA/B-subtype virus in veterinary vaccines.
The virus was introduced through contaminated water supplies.
It was transmitted directly from infected birds to humans.
items.explanation: Genetic sequencing suggested that the virus from the outbreak originated from residual 'un-inactivated' IA/B-subtype virus in veterinary vaccines.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 7)

items.question: Which vaccine was used to terminate the VEE outbreak in Texas?
options: An inactivated vaccine prepared from Everglades virus.
A live-attenuated vaccine developed for human use by the U.S. Army.
A formalin-inactivated vaccine used to protect laboratory workers.
items.explanation: The outbreak was terminated in Texas with a live-attenuated vaccine (TC-83), originally developed for human use by the U.S. Army.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 7)

items.question: What percentage of clinical cases during the VEE epizootics had neurologic symptoms or signs?

items.question: Which virus is transmitted via a similar cycle to western equine encephalitis in the same regions?

items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · ENCEPHALITIS (part 7)

items.question: Which rodent subfamily is primarily associated with the transmission of orthohantaviruses causing HPS in the United States?

items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · PULMONARY DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: Which virus is most commonly implicated in human-to-human transmission of HPS?

items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · PULMONARY DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: What are the typical initial symptoms of Hantavirus pulmonary syndrome?
options: Fever, malaise, myalgia, and gastrointestinal disturbances
Cough, shortness of breath, chest pain, and fever
Headache, stiff neck, confusion, and sensitivity to light
Abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea, and dehydration

items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · PULMONARY DISEASE (part 1)

items.question: Which diagnostic feature is considered almost diagnostic for HPS in experienced centers?
options: Coincident thrombocytopenia, circulating atypical lymphocytes, and a left shift
items.explanation: In experienced centers, coincident thrombocytopenia, circulating atypical lymphocytes (lymphoblasts), and a left shift are considered almost diagnostic for HPS.

items.question: What is a critical management consideration during the initial hours of pulmonary disease due to arthropod-borne viruses?
options: Aggressive IV fluid therapy should be administered immediately.
Early triage-based monitoring and management of hemodynamic, respiratory, and renal dysfunction are crucial.
Mechanical ventilatory support is unnecessary unless hypoxemia persists for more than 48 hours.
Pulmonary artery catheterization should only be used in patients with confirmed shock.
items.explanation: Early triage-based monitoring and management of hemodynamic, respiratory, and renal dysfunction during the first few hours are critical to improved outcomes.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · PULMONARY DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: Which intervention is often needed for patients with respiratory failure and shock in pulmonary disease due to arthropod-borne viruses?
items.explanation: Mechanical ventilatory support of respiratory failure and shock is often needed.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · PULMONARY DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: What does pulmonary artery catheterization enable in the management of critically ill patients?
options: Guided inotropic and vasopressor support with cautious intravascular volume replacement.
Direct measurement of cardiac output without further interventions.
items.explanation: Pulmonary artery catheterization enables pulmonary capillary wedge pressure-guided inotropic and vasopressor support and cautious intravascular volume replacement.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · PULMONARY DISEASE (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is a common observable abnormality due to dysfunction of local circulation in VHF?

items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 1)

items.question: In VHF, what is the primary target organ in HFRS?
items.explanation: The kidneys are primary targets in HFRS.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 1)

items.question: What is the typical onset of symptoms in most VHF syndromes?
options: Abrupt with nonspecific fever and arthralgia/myalgia
items.explanation: Symptom initiation in the acute phase is typically abrupt with nonspecific fever and arthralgia/myalgia.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following factors contributes to hemodynamic instability in VHF?
items.explanation: Hypotension and shock are indications of generalized circulatory dysfunction contributing to hemodynamic instability.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 1)

items.question: Which specific therapy is available for some viral hemorrhagic fevers?
items.explanation: Specific therapies such as convalescent plasma, IV ribavirin, and live-attenuated vaccines are available for some VHFs.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 2)

items.question: What is the recommended treatment for Argentinian and Bolivian hemorrhagic fevers if given within the first 8 days of illness?
items.explanation: Argentinian and Bolivian hemorrhagic fevers are treated with convalescent plasma if administered within the first 8 days of illness.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 2)

items.question: Which virus has a live-attenuated vaccine that significantly reduced its incidence in endemic areas?
items.explanation: A live-attenuated vaccine for Junin virus has significantly reduced the incidence of Argentinian hemorrhagic fever.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 2)

items.question: What is a common clinical finding in patients with Lassa fever?
items.explanation: Thrombocytopenia is a common clinical finding in patients with Lassa fever.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT typically associated with the onset of illness in viral hemorrhagic fever caused by mammarenaviruses?

items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 3)

items.question: Which laboratory abnormality is NOT commonly seen in LASV infections?
items.explanation: Elevated, not decreased, AST activity is a laboratory abnormality seen in LASV infections.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following factors is NOT associated with fatal outcomes in LASV infections?
items.explanation: The presence of a maculopapular rash is not associated with fatal outcomes in LASV infections.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 3)

items.question: Which statement about ribavirin treatment for LASV is correct?
options: Ribavirin is given by slow IV infusion
Ribavirin is effective without any supportive care

items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 3)

items.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature during the febrile stage of viral hemorrhagic fever?

items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 4)

items.question: During which stage of viral hemorrhagic fever does renal perfusion become compromised leading to oliguria?

items.question: What laboratory finding is NOT typically present in the early stages of viral hemorrhagic fever?

items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 4)

items.question: Which of the following statements about Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever virus (CCHFV) is true?

It causes severe VHF over a wide endemic area.
Domestic animals show clinical symptoms when infected.

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a predictor of fatal outcome in viral hemorrhagic fever?

items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 5)

items.question: What is the mainstay of treatment for viral hemorrhagic fever?

items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 5)

items.question: Which clinical feature is NOT typically associated with severe viral hemorrhagic fever?
options: Severe liver injury resulting in jaundice
items.explanation: Person-to-person transmission has not been documented for viral hemorrhagic fever.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 5)

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a typical clinical manifestation of Rift Valley fever?
options: High case fatality rate within 3–6 days
items.explanation: Person-to-person transmission of RVFV has not been documented.
items.reference: Arthropod Borne and Rodent Borne Virus Infections · VIRAL HEMORRHAGIC FEVER (part 5)

items.question: Which vaccine is recommended for veterinarians working in sub-Saharan Africa to prevent RVF?
